title: What Are the Biological Limits of Microtubule Quantum Effects?… Establishment of the Coherence Utility Criterion
abstract: A study published on bioRxiv introduces a new "coherence utility" criterion required for quantum coherence phenomena within the tubulin networks of microtubules to connect with macroscopic biological functions. Analyzed using open quantum systems and the Thermodynamic Uncertainty Relation (TUR), the results demonstrate that amplifying femtosecond (fs) level coherence to a neural scale would require immense power, exceeding the local GTP energy budget by about five orders of magnitude (100,000 times). This research is not intended to advocate for a specific model of consciousness, but rather establishes a reproducible physical benchmark to evaluate future quantum coherence hypotheses related to the brain and microtubules within strict thermodynamic and physical limits. [Quantum Biology Society] Quantum effects in living organisms are unavoidable at the molecular scale, but a massive temporal gap exists between microscopic molecular dynamics measured in femtoseconds (quadrillionths of a second) and macroscopic biological functions that take microseconds to milliseconds or longer. Overcoming this gap and revealing how quantum phenomena contribute to biological processes is a long-standing challenge in quantum biology. Recently, a study was published that mathematically identifies the physical conditions required for quantum coherence within the microtubules of neural networks to have actual functional relevance. The paper "Beyond Redfield: Thermodynamic Bounds and Non-Perturbative Quantum Dynamics in Tubulin Networks," published on the preprint repository bioRxiv, presents this critical benchmark. A research team consisting of Facundo Firmenich, Pau Firmenich, and León Firmenich from the Centro de Estudios del Sur (CEDESUR) and the Universitat de Barcelona (UB) conducted this rigorous physical verification. ■ Coherence Utility Criterion for Biological Functions The research team modeled the tubulin network of microtubules as an open quantum system interacting with a warm and wet cellular environment. Simulation results confirmed that at physiological temperature (310 K), the equilibrium quantum coherence time of tubulin is only at the level of 13-39 fs (femtoseconds). The team formulated that for this extremely short microscopic coherence to translate into millisecond-scale neural network functions, it must satisfy the coherence utility condition of U=Kτ_coh/τ_func≥1, which requires an extreme quantum-classical amplification process via an amplification factor (K). ■ Thermodynamic Bounds and Verification of the Quantum Brain Hypothesis In particular, calculations applying the Thermodynamic Uncertainty Relation (TUR) indicate that achieving this neural-scale amplification requires a minimum power of approximately 10⁻⁷ W. This is a massive figure that exceeds the local GTP hydrolysis energy budget available to the microtubule tubulin network (about 6 × 10⁻¹³ W) by more than five orders of magnitude (100,000 times). In other words, they thermodynamically proved that it is difficult to explain the process of microtubule quantum effects converting into macroscopic neural signals using existing equilibrium models or simple amplification mechanisms alone. This research is not aimed at advocating or refuting specific quantum consciousness models, such as Orchestrated Objective Reduction (Orch OR). Rather, its greatest significance lies in establishing an objective and reproducible physical benchmark framework that can verify and evaluate various quantum coherence hypotheses of the brain and microtubules, which may be raised in the field of quantum biology in the future, within strict thermodynamic and physical limits. https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.05.10.724047v1

title: The Perry–Carlo Precision Suite: Quantum Coherence, Deterministic Timing, and the Perry Constant
abstract: This three‑document package provides a unified analysis and timing architecture built around Anthony L. Perry’s 2026 hypothesis on microtubule‑level quantum modulation of gamma‑band neural precision. The first document offers a complete structural analysis of Perry’s framework, detailing its empirical foundations, decoherence derivations, collective protection mechanisms, and quantum–classical coupling model. From Perry’s quantitative structure, the Perry Constant Equation is formally extracted as the central scalar relating effective microtubule coherence to changes in gamma‑band timing precision: \[ K = \frac{\Delta P}{C_{\text{eff}}} \] The second document introduces the Reality Console deterministic timing architecture, which defines jitter limits, phase‑locking constraints, coherence propagation rules, and classical failure boundaries. This framework evaluates when classical gamma mechanisms fail and when Perry’s quantum modulation could become functionally relevant, without altering any of Perry’s physics. The third document, 'Compendium', provides formal definitions, glossary entries, and structural diagrams linking Perry’s quantum parameters to the deterministic operators of the Reality Console. Together, these documents form a coherent, testable, and transparent two‑layer framework for studying gamma‑band precision. REALITY CONSOLE OPERATORS O_t — Timing operator (jitter envelope) O_φ — Phase‑locking operator (phase constraints) O_c — Coherence propagation operator O_lim — Classical precision limit operator O_net — Network integration operator In Summary: This suite combines a full technical analysis of Perry’s quantum microtubule framework with a deterministic timing architecture capable of evaluating its functional relevance. Perry’s work provides the quantum parameters; the Reality Console supplies the timing constraints. The Perry Constant Equation sits at the centre, offering a simple, falsifiable link between microtubule coherence and gamma‑band precision. Together, the documents form a clear, disciplined, and experimentally testable foundation for future research into neural timing. In summary:I enjoyed going through Perry’s work because he keeps things grounded, he puts real numbers on everything, and he doesn’t pretend the weak spots aren’t there. Those three things make his framework feel genuinely smart instead of speculative. It’s rare to see someone lay out a risky idea this clearly, and that clarity is what makes his work stand out. title: Quantum consciousness revisited: a review of Orch OR and an introduction to the Quantum Cognition Gradient
abstract: Despite centuries of scientific inquiry, the origin of human consciousness, characterized by self-awareness and the presence of qualia, remains one of the deepest mysteries in neuroscience. Orchestrated Objective Reduction (Orch OR), as proposed by Roger Penrose and Stuart Hameroff, posits that consciousness arises from the objective reduction of quantum superpositions within entangled neural microtubules, triggered by gravitational self-energy. Recent theoretical and biophysical studies have renewed interest in quantum coherence in biological systems, including terahertz vibrational measurements, advances in quantum biology, and computational modeling of microtubule dynamics. This review synthesizes these developments and examines major critiques of Orch OR, particularly the challenge of maintaining quantum coherence in warm, wet biological environments. It further introduces the Quantum Cognition Gradient (QCG), a phenomenological, heuristic framework that extends Orch OR by proposing a scalable relationship between cognitive complexity and the formation, persistence, and coordination of quantum-coherent microtubule states prior to objective reduction, while leaving the underlying Diósi–Penrose collapse mechanism unchanged. Finally, several experimental strategies, including terahertz spectroscopy, entanglement-detection assays, interferometric microscopy, and neuromodulatory perturbation studies, are outlined as potential approaches for empirically evaluating predictions of both Orch OR and the QCG framework. By integrating contemporary developments in quantum biology with a quantitative conceptual framework, this review presents Orch OR and the proposed QCG as testable, hypothesis-generating models for investigating the possible relationship between quantum processes and consciousness.

title: The Perry–Carlo Quantum Coherence Compendium: Deterministic Timing Architecture and the Reconciled Perry Constant Equation
abstract: This compendium presents the reconciled Perry–Carlo framework, linking microtubule‑level quantum coherence to deterministic gamma‑band timing architecture. The work integrates Anthony L. Perry’s 2026 quantum‑modulatory model with a fully formalized operator environment, correcting dimensional inconsistencies, resolving thermal‑window tensions, and establishing a simulation‑ready bridge between quantum coherence factors and classical oscillatory limits. A central contribution of this release is the revised Perry Equation, expressed in its corrected dimensional form: $$K = \frac{\Delta(1/\sigma_t)}{C_{\mathrm{eff}}}$$ where: K is the Perry Constant (ms⁻¹) σ_t is temporal jitter (ms) Δ(1 / σ_t) is the Precision Density Rate (ms⁻¹) C_eff is the effective microtubule coherence factor This formulation enforces strict dimensional accuracy and enables direct operator‑level evaluation inside deterministic timing systems. The compendium package includes: Master Compendium (HTML) — full structured document with reconciled physics, deterministic operators, and execution pseudocode. Master Compendium (TXT) — plain‑text edition for archival, portability, and markup‑free environments. Full Compendium Add‑On (TXT) — extended companion containing the foreword, executive summary, operator quickstart sheet, Perry Constant explainer, glossary, experimental predictions, methods, limitations, and appendices. Together, these documents form a complete, operator‑mapped, simulation‑ready framework for evaluating quantum‑modulatory contributions to gamma‑band precision, including experimental pathways such as NV‑center dendritic sensing, hypothermic precision testing, and microtubule lattice manipulation. Author’s Note This compendium brings Perry’s original coherence model into a fully consistent mathematical and operator‑level form. The adjustments made here are not meant to reinterpret his ideas, but to give them the dimensional stability and runtime structure they needed to be evaluated cleanly. The corrected Perry Equation reflects that intention: preserving the core insight while resolving the gaps that prevented it from being applied directly. The documents in this release are arranged to make the framework easier to read, test, and extend. They formalize the bridge between quantum coherence factors and classical timing limits without altering the underlying physics Perry proposed. My role has been to complete the missing structural pieces so the model can stand as a coherent whole. -matt This release is intended as a clear, structured presentation of Perry’s original insight, now placed in a form that can be evaluated and extended. title: The Brain Prevents Quantum Collapse with Fibonacci Geometry... The Secret to Maintaining Cognitive Function Amidst HIV Neuroinf
abstract: • Published on bioRxiv, elucidating the quantum coherence preservation effect of Fibonacci-structured microtubules during HIV-induced neuroinflammation. • Maintains quantum coherence over 10,000 times (10⁴) longer than standard lattice structures... Confirmed the formation of a "quantum sanctuary" through 4π symmetry. • Demonstrated that living organisms secure computational resilience in quantum information processing through golden ratio (1.618) geometric optimization even in harsh environments. [Quantum Biology Society] How do patients maintain normal cognitive functions despite experiencing severe neuroinflammation and cytokine storms during acute HIV infection? Innovative research has been published revealing that the answer to this long-standing medical mystery, the "cognitive paradox," lies in the Fibonacci geometry of microtubules in the brain and quantum mechanics. Recently, a study titled "Quantum Coherence Preservation in Fibonacci-Structured Microtubules During HIV-Induced Neuroinflammation" was posted on the preprint repository bioRxiv. A.C. Demidont, an independent researcher at Nyx Dynamics, LLC, elucidated this marvelous protective mechanism through five complementary computational models. ■ Geometric Optimization Overcoming Thermal Noise Until now, it was thought that biological systems were irrelevant to macroscopic life phenomena because quantum coherence rapidly collapses (decoherence) due to strong inflammatory responses or thermal noise. However, the researcher focused on the structural arrangement of tubulin proteins (microtubules) that act as the cytoskeleton of brain neurons. Analysis showed that when microtubules possess an architecture scaled with the Fibonacci spiral ratio—like the arrangement of a nautilus shell or sunflower seeds—they maintain a state of quantum coherence up to 10,000 times (10⁴) longer than typical structures through geometric optimization. While a standard lattice structure exhibits an exponential decay pattern (exponent α = 10.102), the Fibonacci structure exhibits a power law decay pattern (exponent α = -1.015), preserving coherence remarkably long. ■ A 4π Symmetry Quantum Sanctuary Created by the Golden Ratio (1.618) The researcher explained that this powerful protective mechanism operates through resonant coupling at the golden ratio (φ=1.618) with self-similarity scaling. In particular, this Fibonacci structure implements a 4π symmetric (720° rotational closure) topology, similar to a Klein bottle, forming a so-called "quantum sanctuary" where quantum information is protected without being destroyed even under extreme inflammatory stress. Simulation results showed that through these geometric characteristics, the Fibonacci structure achieves a highly efficient 74.4% preservation of quantum information, effectively blocking inflammatory decoherence. ■ The Marvelous Computational Resilience of Living Organisms This discovery proves that living organisms do not merely passively endure environmental disturbances and quantum decoherence, but actively utilize nature's geometric beauty (mathematical principles) to secure computational resilience in quantum information processing even amidst lethal viral attacks. The researcher anticipated that this achievement goes beyond solving the paradox of how patients with HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders (HAND) maintain cognitive function during initial infection. In the future, it is expected to propose new therapeutic targets to prevent the collapse seen in neurodegenerative diseases and provide groundbreaking new principles for developing biomimetic quantum technologies.

title: The Cell's Skeleton Acts as a Quantum Antenna... Identification of Superradiance in Mega-Protein Architectures
abstract: A study published in The Journal of Physical Chemistry B demonstrates that tryptophan amino acids within microtubules, which make up the cytoskeleton, form mega quantum networks. Applying quantum optics theory, the research confirmed that tryptophan networks on the scale of tens to hundreds of nanometers are strongly entangled, causing a superradiance phenomenon where they collectively absorb and emit light. This is a surprising discovery showing that living organisms use macroscopic structural arrangements to maintain long-range quantum coherence without heat loss for energy transfer, providing a physical foundation for the future design of innovative biomaterials and drug delivery systems. [Quantum Biology Society] Inside the cells that make up our bodies exist giant protein tubes called microtubules. Until now, microtubules were merely considered the skeleton maintaining cell shape or roads for transporting materials. However, a surprising recent study has revealed that these microtubules act as quantum antennas and information highways transmitting massive amounts of energy at the speed of light, sending a huge shockwave through the academic community. The paper titled "Ultraviolet Superradiance from Mega-Networks of Tryptophan in Biological Architectures," published in The Journal of Physical Chemistry B, shows how the macroscopic structures of cells are perfectly controlled quantum-mechanically. An international joint research team led by Professor Philip Kurian and researcher Nathan S. Babcock of Howard University successfully spearheaded this innovative study. ■ Entangled Amino Acids Create a Giant Quantum Antenna The research team focused on the tryptophan amino acids inside the tubulin proteins that make up microtubules. Tryptophan has fluorescent properties that strongly absorb and emit light in the ultraviolet region. As a result of applying quantum optics theory, it was proven that within biological structures like centrioles or axonal bundles, up to more than 100,000 tryptophan molecules do not exist independently. Instead, they form mega-networks on the scale of tens to hundreds of nanometers and are strongly entangled quantum-mechanically. ■ A Flawless Dance of Light: Superradiance When this entangled tryptophan network absorbs light (photons), individual molecules do not react separately; instead, the entire network cooperates perfectly like a single giant quantum antenna to absorb and emit light. In physics, this is called 'superradiance'. Through mathematical models and fluorescence yield experiments, the researchers proved that a superradiant state amplified by thousands of times compared to the spontaneous emission rate of a single tryptophan is formed. Furthermore, this increase in quantum yield is robustly maintained even in the presence of structural disorder and the thermal equilibrium of the biological environment. This means that living organisms are using the macroscopic structural arrangement of microtubules to protect long-range quantum coherence without heat loss. This groundbreaking discovery strongly suggests the possibility that complex biological phenomena, such as information processing in brain neurons or cell division, are fundamentally driven by quantum optical mechanisms. Furthermore, it is anticipated that imitating this perfect quantum antenna structure devised by nature could lead to massive technological leaps in various fields, such as ultra-high-speed quantum communication using light or the design of innovative, heat-loss-free biomaterials and drug delivery vehicles. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38641327/

title: Is Consciousness the Result of Quantum Computation in the Brain's Neural Network? ... A Review of Penrose's Orch OR Theory
abstract: A study published in Physics of Life Reviews provides a culmination and review of the development over the past 20 years of the theory that "orchestrated objective reduction" (Orch OR) occurring within microtubules in brain neural networks forms consciousness. It asserts that the source of conscious experience is not classical synaptic computation, but coherent quantum processes occurring within collections of microtubules. This landmark paper combines physics and neurobiology to explain how the collapse of the quantum wavefunction (objective reduction) couples with space-time geometry to create moments of conscious awareness. [Quantum Biology Society] Where and how exactly human consciousness arises in the brain remains one of the deepest unresolved challenges in modern science and philosophy. Until now, mainstream neuroscience has explained consciousness as an emergent phenomenon resulting from the complexification of classical electrochemical signal computations through synapses between neurons. However, the 'Orchestrated Objective Reduction' (Orch OR) theory, established by Nobel laureate in physics Roger Penrose and anesthesiologist Stuart Hameroff, overturns this classical approach and argues that the origin of consciousness lies in the extremely microscopic quantum world inside brain nerve cells. The paper "Consciousness in the universe: A review of the 'Orch OR' theory," published in the international journal Physics of Life Reviews, is a landmark study that synthesizes this unconventional theory, which has been developing for over 20 years. ■ Microtubules: The Microchip of Consciousness Where Quantum Computation Occurs The researchers focused on collections of microtubules, which are cylindrical protein structures existing inside neurons. They propose that biologically 'orchestrated' coherent quantum processes occur within these microtubules, and that these quantum processes correlate with and regulate the synaptic and membrane activity of nerve cells. Even in the warm and wet brain environment, the fine structure inside microtubules is the core stage that overcomes environmental decoherence and maintains quantum coherence. ■ Quantum Collapse Meets Space-Time Geometry: The Birth of Conscious Moments The most original part of the Orch OR theory lies in explaining the termination point of continuous Schrödinger evolution—that is, the process of the collapse of the quantum wavefunction—using the concept of objective reduction (OR) in the specific 'Diósi-Penrose (DP) scheme'. It suggests that as quantum entanglement and computation are orchestrated and gradually grow within microtubules, the very instantaneous collapse that occurs when reaching this threshold of objective reduction creates the "moments of conscious awareness and/or choice" that we experience. ■ The Great Integration of Neuroscience and Quantum Physics This paper directly connects the biochemical structure of the brain with quantum mechanics and space-time geometry, the most fundamental physical laws of the universe. Based on the fact that the DP form of objective reduction is deeply related to the basic geometry of space-time, it strongly suggests that a fundamental connection exists between the biomolecular processes of the brain and the basic structure of the universe. This provides a revolutionary insight that human consciousness and free will are not merely mechanical illusions of classical neural networks, but could be intrinsic phenomena of the universe itself. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24070914/

title: 'Quantum Mechanics' Intervenes Even in the Process of Building the Cell Skeleton... Microtubule Radical Pair Mechanism Identifi
abstract: • Analyzed the effects of weak magnetic fields and the magnesium isotope (25Mg) on tubulin polymerization dynamics. • Confirmed changes attributed to nuclear spin properties rather than mass differences... Perfectly explained by the radical pair mechanism (RPM). • Demonstrated that quantum effects are involved in broad biological mechanisms, such as cell structure formation, beyond avian magnetoreception. [Quantum Biology Society] The biological effects of weak magnetic fields and isotopes, which could not be explained by classical thermodynamic models, have finally been unveiled within the framework of quantum mechanics. Recently, a study titled "Magnetic Field-dependent Isotope Effect Supports Radical Pair Mechanism in Tubulin Polymerization" was published on the biology preprint repository bioRxiv. This study, conducted by a joint research team including the University of Waterloo, is a groundbreaking achievement demonstrating the universality of quantum effects across biological phenomena. ■ The Magic of 'Nuclear Spin' Regulating Cytoskeleton Formation The research team focused on the polymerization process of the 'tubulin' protein, a core component of microtubules that act as the intracellular skeleton. By substituting the magnesium isotope (25Mg), which possesses a nuclear spin, and applying a weak magnetic field of approximately 3 mT (millitesla), they precisely analyzed how the polymerization dynamics of tubulin changed through experiments and simulations. As a result, dynamic changes that could not be explained by simple mass differences between isotopes were observed. In other words, it was confirmed that the pure nuclear spin property of the isotope directly affects the speed and pattern at which tubulin proteins bind and assemble. ■ Beyond the Migratory Bird's Compass: 'Quantum Biology' Expanded to Overall Biological Phenomena The researchers stated that this magnetic field-dependent isotope effect can only be perfectly explained through the radical pair mechanism (RPM). The radical pair mechanism is a representative quantum biological phenomenon in which the spin state of an electron pair, formed when two molecules exchange electrons, is influenced by external weak magnetic fields or the spins of surrounding atomic nuclei, thereby overturning the outcome of the overall chemical reaction. Until now, the radical pair mechanism was widely known primarily as the principle behind the quantum compass (magnetoreception) by which migratory birds sense the Earth's magnetic field to navigate. However, this study has a significant impact as it demonstrated that radical pair-based quantum effects are directly involved in broad and essential biological mechanisms, such as the formation of cellular structures and functional regulation, which are the most fundamental units of life. It is expected to be a groundbreaking milestone showing that an intricate quantum mechanical control system, previously unknown to us, is operating at the foundation of biological phenomena.

title: Computational Analysis of Microtubule-Mediated Saltatory Neuroelectrical Transmission: A Theoretical Exploration
abstract: It was recently postulated that neural microtubules (neuro-MTs), which are densely packed inside axons and dendrites, are vacuum cylindrical nanotubes that can mediate neuroelectrical transmission with a unique form of quasi-superconductivity. In this work, the behaviors of free electrons inside a theoretical neuro-MT are modeled using computational analysis and calculations. We reveal that neuro-MTs can function as nanosized physiological devices that mediate neuroelectrical transmission with a super-high energy efficiency in a quasi-superconducting manner. Under physiologically relevant conditions, the binding of cytosolic cations (e.g., K+ and Na+) to the surface residues of a neuro-MT triggers its transition from a resting state to an active state, and the rapid dissociation of these cations triggers the opposite. The dipole ring structures of a neuro-MT will help terminate the free electron conduction inside the vacuum tunnel with high efficiency. The proposed neuro-MT-mediated electrical transmission offers a potential mechanistic explanation for the saltatory conduction of action potentials along an axon or a dendrite. This theoretical exploration also offers unique insights into the rational design of biomimetic room-temperature quasi-superconducting materials, such as carbon or silicone-based quasi-superconducting nanotubes.

title: Quantum Coherence Surviving at Room Temperature... Breakthrough Opened with MOF Mimicking Biological Photosynthesis
abstract: • Published in Science Advances, demonstrating that the quantum coherence of entangled multiexcitons is maintained at room temperature, not cryogenic temperatures, using a metal-organic framework (MOF). • Successfully strongly protected the coherence of the quintet triplet pair state (⁵TT) by suppressing the random motions of molecules within an ordered framework integrated with chromophores. • Proposes a groundbreaking material design direction for realizing entangled multi-qubit-based quantum technologies that operate even in room-temperature environments by applying the principles of biological multiexciton dynamics. [Quantum Biology Society] The biggest barrier that must be overcome for the commercialization of quantum computing and quantum sensing technologies is "temperature." Implementing a state of quantum coherence at room temperature, which previously could barely be maintained only in cryogenic environments, has been a long-standing aspiration of the physics community. Recently, inspired by the principles of biological photosynthesis, a study that successfully implemented room-temperature quantum coherence inside a metal-organic framework (MOF) was published, drawing the attention of the academic community. A paper titled "Room-temperature quantum coherence of entangled multiexcitons in a metal-organic framework" was published in the international journal Science Advances, presenting the answer to this fundamental challenge. A joint research team, including Professor Nobuhiro Yanai from Kyushu University and Professor Yasuhiro Kobori from Kobe University in Japan, successfully led this groundbreaking empirical study. ■ Singlet Fission Beyond the Limits of Cryogenics The singlet fission phenomenon is evaluated as having tremendous potential for implementing entangled multi-qubit systems even at room temperature because it can generate an exchange-coupled quintet triplet pair state (⁵TT) after absorbing light. Until now, however, the quantum coherence of ⁵TT was easily broken even by minute thermal fluctuations, strictly limiting its observation to cryogenic environments. ■ The MOF Framework Confining Molecular Motion After exploring what kind of material design would enable room-temperature coherence, the research team utilized a metal-organic framework (MOF) material in which chromophores are precisely integrated. The ordered and rigid framework of the MOF effectively suppresses the random thermal motions of the molecules inside. As a result of the analysis, it was confirmed that inside the MOF, where molecular fluctuations are strictly controlled, the quantum coherence of the ⁵TT derived from singlet fission is strongly maintained for over 100 nanoseconds (ns) even at room temperature. This is a groundbreaking quantum protection mechanism showing that microscopic quantum states can be protected from the interference of macroscopic thermal energy. ■ A Leap Towards the Era of Room-Temperature Quantum Computing and Sensing This study is a representative case of successfully applying the principles of biological multiexciton dynamics to artificial metal-organic framework materials. Experts evaluate this achievement as a major advancement that breaks through the limitations of room-temperature quantum technology, providing decisive clues for implementing quantum computing and ultra-sensitive quantum sensors by stably utilizing entangled multiple qubits even at room temperature.

title: Light Waving in a Room-Temperature Biological Environment... First Demonstration of Photosynthetic Quantum Coherence
abstract: A study published in PNAS in 2010 demonstrated for the first time that quantum coherence within the photosynthetic complex (FMO), previously confirmed only at cryogenic temperatures of millikelvin levels, is maintained even at physiological temperatures (277 K, 4 degrees Celsius). Utilizing state-of-the-art two-dimensional (2D) electronic spectroscopy, it was confirmed that light energy is transferred in the form of a quantum mechanical wave-like motion even in a warm and complex biological protein environment, remaining intact for at least 300 femtoseconds (fs). This is a monumental achievement suggesting that living organisms are highly evolved quantum machines that achieve near 100% transmission efficiency by actively utilizing the thermal vibrations of proteins, going beyond simply enduring thermal decoherence. [Quantum Biology Society] Photosynthesis, the process by which plants and bacteria absorb sunlight and convert it into energy, boasts a phenomenal energy transmission efficiency of nearly 100%. While it was revealed that the secret to this overwhelming efficiency is that light energy is transferred in the form of a quantum mechanical wave rather than a particle, simultaneously exploring multiple pathways, this phenomenon was initially confirmed only in a limited way in cryogenic laboratory environments at millikelvin levels. Recently, however, a surprising study proving that this delicate quantum phenomenon operates intact even within the warm and complex internal environment of actual living organisms was published, drawing the attention of the global scientific community. The paper titled "Long-lived quantum coherence in photosynthetic complexes at physiological temperature," published in the international journal PNAS (Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences) in 2010, is a landmark study showing that photosynthetic organisms are practically governed by quantum mechanics. A research team led by Professor Gregory S. Engel and Gitt Panitchayangkoon at the University of Chicago spearheaded this monumental demonstration. ■ Quantum Coherence Surviving in a Room-Temperature Biological Environment The research team applied state-of-the-art 2D electronic spectroscopy technology to the FMO (Fenna-Matthews-Olson) complex, which acts as a photosynthetic antenna for green sulfur bacteria. As a result of precise measurements, they demonstrated for the first time in the world that even in the warm and wet protein environment at physiological temperatures (4 degrees Celsius, 277 K) where actual life forms operate—rather than at cryogenic temperatures—the quantum coherence state, in which light energy vibrates in the form of a quantum mechanical wave, is clearly maintained without being destroyed for at least 300 femtoseconds (one trillionth of a second). ■ Nature's Wisdom of Reversely Utilizing Thermal Noise Generally, in a hot and complex environment, random collisions of particles cause thermal decoherence, where the quantum state breaks down instantly. However, the results of this study suggest that living organisms do not simply passively endure this thermal noise. Rather, the photosynthetic proteins were found to protect the fragile quantum coherence through correlated motions within the protein matrix surrounding the chromophores. In other words, nature has been operating as a highly evolved quantum machine to eliminate friction in the energy transfer process and achieve ultimate energy transmission efficiency. This monumental discovery firmly established the core paradigm of quantum biology—that quantum mechanics underlies life phenomena—and triggered explosive research in the academic community. Furthermore, by mimicking nature's perfect quantum design, it provides immense physical inspiration for developing next-generation ultra-high-efficiency solar cells or quantum computing devices that operate without energy loss even in disordered and dynamic room-temperature environments. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/artic

title: The Secret of the 24-Hour Biological Clock... Quantum Entanglement and Coherence Synchronize the Entire Body
abstract: A study published in Frontiers in Physiology proposed an immediate and systemic synchronization mechanism of the circadian rhythm as a convergence model of 'chronobiology' and 'quantum biology'. It elucidated the phenomenon of temperature compensation and ultra-high-speed systemic synchronization, which could not be explained solely by conventional transcription-translation chemical reaction feedback loops, through a quantum network model utilizing the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the brain as a 'quantum hub'. It proved that the macroscopic biological clock is maintained by microscopic quantum entanglement through three non-classical mechanisms: quantum coherence mediated by biophotons, electromagnetic field synchronization of cryptochrome proteins, and quantum tunneling in neural synapses. [Quantum Biology Society] The circadian clock, which regulates the organic physiological activities of living organisms in accordance with the Earth's rotation, is a core mechanism for sustaining life. However, the immediate and unified systemic synchronization that occurs from the central clock in the brain down to the peripheral tissues and numerous cells throughout the body has been physically difficult to fully explain in terms of speed and time scale relying solely on classical biochemical models that depend on molecular diffusion and collisions. The paper titled "Circadian system coordination: new perspectives beyond classical models," recently published in the international journal Frontiers in Physiology, solved the secret of this macroscopic time synchronization from a quantum mechanical perspective through an innovative convergence of chronobiology and quantum biology. A joint research team led by Ovidiu Constantin Baltatu of Alfaisal University in Saudi Arabia and Anhembi Morumbi University in Brazil, along with Professors Luciana Aparecida Campos and José Cipolla-Neto of the University of São Paulo, published this disruptive research. ■ The Suprachiasmatic Nucleus (SCN) Operating as a Quantum Hub The research team pointed out the limitations of existing models, which explain rhythmic changes in gene expression and protein levels, in accounting for how precise timing is maintained despite intracellular thermal noise or body temperature fluctuations (the phenomenon of temperature compensation). To overcome this, the research team proposed a novel network model in which the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN), the master clock of the brain, acts as a 'quantum hub' to form a state of quantum entanglement with peripheral clocks throughout the body. According to this model, similar to the physical concept that macroscopic biological time emerges through entanglement between two quantum systems (the PaW mechanism), consistent temporal coherence is maintained across the entire biological network through quantum entanglement between the SCN and peripheral tissues. ■ Three Quantum Mechanisms Synchronizing Macroscopic Time The research team presented three specific physical mechanisms that mediate the transfer of quantum states between the SCN and peripheral tissues. First, ultra-weak 'biophotons' emitted from SCN neurons instantaneously transmit long-distance information, surpassing classical signal transmission speeds, through quantum coherence. Second, minute electromagnetic field oscillations generated by the SCN interact with the spin state of the flavin-superoxide radical pair inside the 'cryptochrome' protein, inducing synchronization even amidst microscopic environmental changes. Third, quantum tunneling occurring during processes such as synaptic vesicle release ensures consistent neurotransmission speeds even amidst physical fluctuations in body temperature. The convergence of these three quantum mechanical mechanisms explains the phenomenal efficiency with which living organisms synchronize the time of their entire body without a single margin of error, even amidst extreme fluctuations in their internal environment.
