title: Quantum Coherence in Photosystem II: Environment-Assisted Transport and the Role of Vibronic Coupling in Biological Light-Harvesting
abstract: A review synthesizing spectroscopic evidence and theoretical frameworks for quantum coherence in photosynthetic light-harvesting, with emphasis on Photosystem II's oxygen-evolving complex as a uniquely constrained testbed for resolving open questions in quantum biology.

title: Replicating the Quantum Coherence of the Eye in an Artificial Molecule... The Dawn of the Quantum Biomimetic Era
abstract: A study published in Nature Communications has successfully implemented and demonstrated for the first time vibrational coherence—the core of the ultrafast and highly efficient photoreaction of rhodopsin, the protein responsible for animal vision—in a synthetic molecular device. Through sub-10 femtosecond (fs) time-resolved pump-probe spectroscopy, the research proved that an artificial synthetic molecule (the MeO-NAIP switch), just like natural proteins, maintains distinct quantum coherence that remains unbroken even after transitioning from the excited state to the ground state after absorbing light. By combining quantum chemical simulations to mathematically elucidate how coherent nuclear motion depends on the molecule's fine geometric and electronic structures, the study presents a groundbreaking roadmap for the development of artificial nanodevices and novel drug platforms that mimic nature's sophisticated quantum biological phenomena. [Quantum Biology Society] In the fleeting moment we see light and experience vision, a marvelous quantum mechanical event occurs inside the rhodopsin protein in the retina of the eye. Instead of molecules moving randomly upon absorbing light, all atoms synchronize and change their structure instantly while maintaining a state of vibrational quantum coherence, much like a perfectly coordinated orchestra. Thanks to this mechanism, rhodopsin can convert light energy into mechanical energy (visual signals) with remarkably high efficiency and without heat loss. What if we could artificially replicate this perfect quantum system of living organisms? The paper titled "Engineering the vibrational coherence of vision into a synthetic molecular device," recently published in the international journal Nature Communications, has turned this dreamlike imagination into reality. This research, led by a joint team (including Massimo Olivucci, Jérémie Léonard, and others) from the Université de Strasbourg in France, Bowling Green State University in the USA, and the Università di Siena in Italy, demonstrates a perfect fusion of quantum biology and artificial nanotechnology. ■ Transplanting Nature's Quantum Switch into an Artificial Molecule The research team designed and synthesized MeO-NAIP (compound Z-1), a biomimetic synthetic molecule that precisely mimics the π-electron system and geometric constraints of the protonated Schiff base of retinal (PSBR), the core chromophore inside rhodopsin. They then observed how this artificial molecule responds to light using vibrational coherence spectroscopy with an ultrashort 8.5-femtosecond (one femtosecond is one quadrillionth of a second) pump pulse. The results were astounding. Just like natural rhodopsin, the artificial synthetic molecule underwent ultrafast photoisomerization, instantly changing its shape through a conical intersection without an energy barrier upon receiving light. More importantly, the study clearly demonstrated that the nuclear vibrational quantum coherence with a frequency of about 80cm⁻¹, initiated in the molecule's excited state (S₁) during this process, is not destroyed but preserved intact even after the photoreaction ends and the photoproduct drops to the ground state (S₀). ■ Minute Structural Differences Turn Quantum Coherence On and Off Furthermore, through advanced quantum chemical simulations (such as CASPT2), the research team mathematically analyzed what happens when the methyl group at the C5 position of this artificial molecular structure (Z-1) is slightly replaced with a hydrogen atom (compound E-2). As a result, they revealed that with just a very minor chemical modification, the molecule changes to a nearly planar structure and its excited-state energy landscape is altered, causing it to lose the targeted coherence and collapse into random, diffusive motion. This serves as disruptive evidence that paradoxically shows how perfectly nature has set the geometric and electronic structures of molecules through evolution to optim

title: The Secret of Photosynthetic Efficiency: Multipartite Quantum Entanglement Inside Proteins
abstract: A study published on arXiv has strictly demonstrated the existence of true multipartite quantum entanglement between bacteriochlorophyll-a (BChl a) molecules within the Fenna-Matthews-Olson (FMO) light-harvesting complex of green sulfur bacteria. Using entanglement measures in the single-excitation subspace (bipartite concurrence and a global entanglement measure), the study proved for the first time in a biological system that these entanglement states persist for approximately 2 picoseconds (ps) not only at cryogenic temperatures but also at physiological temperatures (300 K, room temperature), surviving macroscopically. The findings suggest that strong quantum entanglement between distant molecules can enhance the robustness and efficiency of the energy transfer network as the exciton moves toward the reaction center, even amidst environmental noise (decohering effects). [Quantum Biology Society] Photosynthesis, known as one of the most efficient energy conversion processes in nature, has long been a subject of wonder for scientists. While previous research focused on quantum coherence at the single-particle level, a groundbreaking study has recently been published mathematically proving that "true quantum entanglement"—the non-local correlation between multiple pigment molecules—actually operates within living organisms, drawing global attention. The paper titled "Quantum entanglement in photosynthetic light harvesting complexes," published in the preprint repository arXiv, is evaluated as a monumental achievement in elucidating macroscopic quantum phenomena in biological systems. An international joint research team led by Mohan Sarovar, Akihito Ishizaki, Graham R. Fleming, and K. Birgitta Whaley from UC Berkeley and Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (LBNL) in the USA spearheaded this innovative research. ■ Demonstration of Multipartite Quantum Entanglement within the FMO Complex The researchers focused on the FMO (Fenna-Matthews-Olson) light-harvesting complex of green sulfur bacteria (Chlorobium tepidum). By precisely analyzing the exciton dynamics between the pigment molecules within the complex using a non-Markovian quantum master equation, they proved through bipartite concurrence and a measure of global entanglement that true multipartite quantum entanglement is formed, going beyond simple individual coherence. This marks the first rigorous instance of quantifying quantum entanglement in a biological system. ■ Quantum Information Maintained Through Room-Temperature Noise The most surprising discovery is that these delicate quantum entanglement states survive meaningfully even at warm and wet physiological temperatures. While it was generally thought that quantum states are instantly destroyed by the thermal fluctuations of the surrounding proteins and solvent (decohering effects), the study's results confirmed that the natural FMO complex maintains bipartite entanglement between dimerized pigments and even a small amount of long-range and multipartite entanglement at 300 K. This entanglement plays a crucial role in enhancing the efficiency and robustness of the energy transfer network as the exciton travels to the reaction center. ■ Living Organisms as Optimized Quantum Machines This study suggests that living organisms are not merely losing quantum phenomena in harsh environmental noise but are rather highly designed, optimized quantum machines that endure and actively utilize them. Mimicking these perfect energy transfer mechanisms of nature is expected to provide key physical clues for maximizing the efficiency of next-generation solar cells or developing innovative quantum communication and metrology devices that operate robustly even at room temperature. https://arxiv.org/abs/0905.3787

title: Adaptive low-rank variational quantum algorithm for simulating dissipative dynamics in photosynthetic complexes
abstract: Simulating open quantum system dynamics via exact density-matrix propagation faces exponential complexity, scaling as [Formula: see text] and becoming intractable beyond approximately 15 chromophores on classical hardware-despite specialised approaches such as the Hierarchy Of Pure States (HOPS), Meso-HOPS, and multi-Davydov variational methods having extended tractability for specific regimes. We develop an adaptive low-rank variational quantum algorithm (LR-VQA) achieving polynomial scaling [Formula: see text] through singular value decomposition-based tensor compression and dissipation-engineered cost functions, providing a quantum-hardware-compatible framework complementary to existing classical techniques. Benchmarking on Fenna-Matthews-Olson (FMO) complexes spanning 5-12 chromophores yields mean fidelities 0.87-0.95 across 50 independent trials with 95 % confidence intervals below 0.001, validated by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) ([Formula: see text]). Noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) device viability is demonstrated through realistic simulations using IBM Heron noise specifications, achieving fidelity [Formula: see text] for the 7-site complex. Comparative scaling analysis reveals a computational crossover at [Formula: see text] chromophores, beyond which LR-VQA provides the sole quantum-hardware-compatible tractable simulation pathway while exact density-matrix methods become computationally prohibitive. Agreement with experimental energy transfer timescales within 3 % validates physical accuracy. A complete open-source implementation achieves sub-90-second runtimes. This framework opens a pathway toward quantum advantage in quantum biology with natural extensions to non-Markovian dynamics and experimental photosynthetic antenna systems containing 50-300 chromophores.

title: Encompassing Everything in Quantum Biology... A Comprehensive Guide to the Physical Principles Leading Nanomedicine and Bio-Inn
abstract: • A technical monograph on quantum biology, synthesizing an extensive 161 pages and 1,754 references, has been published on the arXiv repository. • Establishes a unified theoretical framework based on 'open quantum systems' for fragmented quantum biology research, including exciton coherence in photosynthesis, biological electron transfer, and microtubule vibrations. • Connects microscopic quantum phenomena with macroscopic biological processes, providing an essential and comprehensive guide for the future development of nanomedicine and innovative biotechnology. [Quantum Biology Society] A technical monograph perfectly synthesizing the vast history and physical principles of Quantum Biology, which investigates the origins of life phenomena from a quantum mechanical perspective, has been published, drawing significant attention from the academic community. Recently, "Physical Principles of Quantum Biology," co-authored by Dr. Nathan S. Babcock and Brandy N. Babcock, was released on the preprint repository arXiv. This document, the result of analyzing 1,754 cited references over 161 pages, provides a fundamental and unified foundation for quantum biology to move beyond fragmented curiosity and establish itself as a firm modern scientific discipline. ■ A 'Unified Framework' Binding Fragmented Research Together Until now, individual research in quantum biology has exploded across various topics, such as exciton coherence in photosynthesis, magnetic field sensing in birds (radical pair mechanism), ultraweak photon emission, and quantum vibrations in microtubules. However, the academic barrier to entry was high due to the lack of a unified theoretical framework or textbook penetrating the entire field, and there was no clear consensus even among researchers regarding the academic scope of quantum biology. To solve this problem, this monograph deeply illuminated living organisms as 'open quantum systems' that constantly exchange energy and information with their environment. It presents a new physical framework that goes beyond the limitations of conventional closed and isolated quantum mechanics. In particular, moving past phenomena based on simple electrostatic models, it strictly classified and defined how the non-trivial quantum coherence and entanglement of biomolecules enable the dynamical functions of life. ■ A New Horizon for Disease Treatment and Nanomedicine The latter half of the book intensively explores how these quantum biological principles can be practically applied to medicine and biotechnology in the future. The researchers conducted an in-depth analysis of quantum spin control of intracellular mitochondria and reactive oxygen species (ROS), as well as cell signaling systems utilizing the optical and electromagnetic vibrations of microtubules. Based on this, they covered a wide range of clinical and technological possibilities leading to photodynamic therapy, electromagnetic field therapy, and regenerative medicine of cells and tissues. The fact that living organisms do not lose quantum effects even in so-called warm, wet, and noisy harsh environments, but rather actively utilize them to achieve high-efficiency information processing and energy conversion, provides crucial inspiration for designing artificial photosynthetic devices, quantum sensors, and nano-bio hybrid materials in the future. Through this literature, the authors define that 'quantum biology goes beyond the limits of isolated quantum systems to ask how life phenomena ultimately operate in complex environments'. They emphasized that "it will be a key guide not only for academic researchers but also for medical technologists, biochemists, and physicists to rigorously understand the quantum foundation of life and develop it into practical nano-bio application technologies".

title: Plants Endure Stress Through Quantum Mechanics: From Photosynthesis and Magnetosensing to Enzyme Catalysis and Oxidative Stress
abstract: Published in the Elsevier journal Plant Stress, this review synthesizes the field by extending the scope of plant quantum biology beyond the traditional territory of photosynthesis and magnetosensing to enzyme catalysis and stress responses (light, oxidative, temperature, and biotic stress). It offers a broad survey of how four quantum phenomena—quantum coherence, entanglement, the radical pair mechanism, and quantum tunneling—may be involved in light-harvesting efficiency, magnetic-field sensing, and enzyme reaction rates. It projects that understanding and controlling these quantum effects could be harnessed for sustainable agriculture, such as developing stress-tolerant crops, while also pointing to the challenges posed by biological complexity and experimental limitations. [Quantum Biology Society] Plant biology has traditionally explained stress responses in the language of classical physics and biochemistry. But as evidence accumulates that quantum phenomena once thought to be the exclusive province of the microscopic world are in fact involved in the activities of life, quantum biology—which explores what role quantum mechanics (QM) plays in core functions such as photosynthesis, light perception, and stress responses—is emerging as a new field. Concepts like superposition, entanglement, tunneling, and coherence are its central tools. A review recently published in the Elsevier journal Plant Stress, "Plant quantum biology: The quantum dimension of plant responses to stress," surveys this field broadly. Massimo E. Maffei of the University of Turin in Italy extends the scope of plant quantum biology—until now discussed largely in terms of photosynthesis and magnetosensing—to enzyme catalysis, stress responses, and, further still, agricultural applications. At the same time, the author is careful to note that the field is still young and that established evidence and theoretical hypotheses remain intermingled, drawing a cautious map of the terrain. ■ Photosynthesis: Quantum Coherence That Captures Light The area where the firmest evidence has accumulated in plant quantum biology is photosynthesis. Photosynthetic organisms convert the photons they absorb into chemical energy with almost no loss, achieving a quantum efficiency approaching 100%. The energy of a light-harvesting complex that has absorbed light is delivered to the reaction center within extraordinarily short times—femtoseconds to picoseconds—and this efficiency and speed are difficult to explain by classical physics alone. The phenomenon drawing attention here is quantum coherence. The excitation energy (exciton) generated by photon absorption is not trapped on a single pigment molecule but exists as a wave-like superposition spread across several pigments, so that it can explore multiple routes to the reaction center simultaneously. In the FMO complex of green sulfur bacteria, long-lived coherence persisting even at physiological temperatures has been observed by two-dimensional electronic spectroscopy (2DES), and the protein scaffold is thought to play a role in shielding this coherence from external disturbance. Quantum entanglement between pigments has also been proposed as a way to coordinate exciton movement and aid energy transfer, but direct evidence in photosynthetic systems is not yet as solid as that for coherence. Further, active debate continues over whether this coherence plays a functional role in genuinely improving energy-transfer efficiency or is merely an incidental byproduct of the structure, and over whether it is electronic coherence or vibronic (vibrational–electronic) coherence. On this point, the author presents both supportive and critical views in balance. ■ Magnetosensing: Radical Pairs That Read the Earth's Magnetic Field Plants respond even to fields as weak as the Earth's geomagnetic field, with effects on growth, development, circadian rhythms, and even the direction in which roots grow. The leading framework for explaining this

title: Quantum Mechanics Governs Animal Behavior... Non-canonical Radical Pair Compass Overturns Existing Orthodoxy
abstract: A study published in Nature presents in vivo experimental results overturning the canonical radical pair mechanism orthodoxy that the cryptochrome (CRY) protein-FAD-tryptophan chain as a complete set is required for animal magnetosensitivity. Through neuro-electrophysiology and circadian rhythm analysis in Drosophila, the study demonstrated for the first time that the 52 C-terminal amino acid residues of the CRY protein alone, with the FAD-binding domain and tryptophan chain completely removed, are sufficient to maintain magnetosensitivity. By revealing that merely increasing the concentration of free FAD inside the cell, unbound to protein, elicits a magnetic field response, the study offers a groundbreaking biophysical insight that the magnetic sensor and signal transducer in animals do not necessarily have to be the same molecule. [Quantum Biology Society] The principle of the biological compass by which animals such as migratory birds and insects sense the Earth's minute magnetic field to navigate has long been a major mystery in the scientific community. The most prevailing hypothesis explaining this phenomenon has been that an FAD coenzyme and a tryptophan amino acid chain exchange electrons within the light-absorbing cryptochrome (CRY) protein to form a quantum-entangled radical pair. Recently, however, a study utilizing Drosophila (fruit flies) has completely shattered the framework of this firmly established canonical mechanism, proving that life's quantum compass operates much more flexibly and marvelously than previously imagined. The paper titled "Essential elements of radical pair magnetosensitivity in Drosophila," published in the international journal Nature, reported groundbreaking empirical results that will change the paradigm of quantum biology. ■ A Quantum Compass That Operates Without a Tryptophan Chain A multinational joint research team, including researchers from the University of Manchester and the University of Leicester in the UK, precisely measured magnetosensitivity through the electrophysiological responses (action potential firing) of aCC motorneurons and changes in the circadian period of individual flies. According to existing theory, if the tryptophan chain and the FAD-binding site—the core pathways for forming radical pairs—are destroyed, the ability to sense magnetic fields should be lost. However, when the research team completely removed these two core structures and expressed only the remaining 52 C-terminal amino acid residues (Luc-CT) in the flies, surprisingly, the flies responded normally to blue light and external magnetic fields (MF), fully retaining their behavioral and neurophysiological magnetosensitivity. This provides conclusive evidence that the prerequisites of the existing radical pair theory are not essential. ■ Separation of Sensor and Transducer: Rediscovery of Free FAD Taking a step further, the research team artificially increased the concentration of free FAD floating in the cytoplasm without binding to proteins. As a result, even in the absence of the intact cryptochrome protein, it was observed that high levels of FAD alone allowed nerve cells to react sensitively to magnetic fields. This series of results shows that animal magnetosensing is not controlled entirely by a single macromolecule called cryptochrome. In other words, it strongly suggests that FAD, a common intracellular redox coenzyme, may have evolved to independently form quantum radical pairs to act as a sensor for magnetic fields, while the tail portion (C-terminus) of cryptochrome acts as a transducer that amplifies and converts this into a biological signal. This study is a monumental achievement clearly proving in vivo that living organisms perceive the external world by utilizing flexible quantum spin dynamics within cells, without being bound to specific protein structures. This discovery, which re-establishes how the complex behavioral biology of animals is governed so precisely and flexibly by pu

title: The Secret of the Migratory Bird's Compass: The Elaborate Dance of Composite Radical Pairs, Proteins, and Solvents
abstract: · Published in the Journal of the American Chemical Society (JACS), the stabilization mechanism of radical pairs in avian cryptochrome (ErCry4a) was elucidated using hybrid quantum mechanical/molecular mechanical (QM/MM) simulations. · Confirmed that the reorganization of the protein and aqueous solvent strongly stabilizes the long-range charge-transfer states between the tryptophan cation and flavin anion. · Proposed the novel "composite" radical pair model for the first time, where two tryptophans share the charge simultaneously rather than acting as a single entity. [Quantum Biology Society] The secret of the biological compass, which allows migratory birds to navigate accurately over thousands of kilometers, has been further unveiled through a convergence of microscopic quantum mechanics and molecular dynamics. This resolves the long-standing question of how quantum spin states within the cryptochrome proteins in avian eyes can endure the noisy biological environment long enough to detect the Earth's magnetic field. Recently, a paper titled "Protein and Solvent Reorganization Drives Radical Pair Stability in Avian Cryptochrome 4a" was published in the Journal of the American Chemical Society (JACS), drawing significant attention from the academic community. A multinational collaborative research team, including Jiate Luo, Sharon Hammes-Schiffer, and Ilia A. Solov'yov, elucidated the stabilization mechanism of this biological compass through their study. ■ Reorganization of Protein and Solvent Guarding the Biological Compass When the cryptochrome-4a (ErCry4a) protein, found in the retina of the night-migratory European robin (Erithacus rubecula), absorbs blue light, it triggers stepwise electron transfers along a tryptophan tetrad to the internal flavin, forming a radical pair. The subtle electron spin state of this radical pair responds to the Earth's magnetic field to sense direction. To understand the principles by which these quantum spins remain stable without collapsing, the research team employed advanced computational chemistry techniques, including first-principles electronic structure methods and hybrid quantum mechanical/molecular mechanical (QM/MM) simulations. The analysis revealed that following the electron transfer, the reorganization of the protein's internal structure and the surrounding aqueous solvent molecules strongly stabilizes the radical pairs. In other words, the protein does not merely act as a passive scaffold for the reaction; instead, it interacts dynamically with surrounding water molecules to serve as a safety net, protecting the long-range charge-transfer states between the flavin anion radical and the tryptophan cation radical from losing energy. ■ Discovery of the "Composite" Radical Pair that Breaks Conventional Wisdom The most notable finding in this study is the validation, through hybrid simulation analysis, of a "composite" radical pair that goes beyond the conventional simple 1:1 radical pair model. The research team provided physicochemical evidence that the tryptophans along the electron transfer pathway do not act individually; rather, two amino acids share the charge simultaneously, forming a single, massive composite cation radical. This charge-sharing phenomenon disperses the radical's energy, enhancing stability, and reveals that this composite state acts as the functional magnetoreceptor, maximizing the lifetime of the quantum spins. This research is hailed as a groundbreaking achievement that clearly defines the mechanism by which biological proteins strategically extend the lifetime of quantum spins, even in a warm and complex biological environment, through elaborate dynamical interactions with the surrounding solvent. Experts anticipate that this discovery will not only deepen our understanding of the quantum biological magnetoreception mechanism in migratory birds but also provide crucial inspiration for the future design of ultra-precise quantum sensors and biomimetic n

title: Unraveling the Secret of the Quantum Compass in the Eyes of Migratory Birds... Pioneering Establishment of the Radical Pair Mec
abstract: A study published in the Biophysical Journal is a core theoretical paper that detailed and formulated the radical pair mechanism driven by light absorption as the principle of avian magnetic sensing. It presents a 3D mathematical model showing that electron radical pairs generated by light inside photoreceptor proteins present in the avian eye (retina) oscillate between singlet and triplet spin states depending on the direction of the Earth's magnetic field. It proposes the theory that the difference in reaction yields of the spin states is converted into a macroscopic biochemical signal, superimposing the direction of the magnetic field as a pattern on vision, thereby providing the physical foundation for the development of modern quantum biology. [Quantum Biology Society] The question of how migratory birds sense the Earth's minute magnetic field and use it for navigation has been a long-standing mystery in the biological community. To solve this mystery, the study "A Model for Photoreceptor-Based Magnetoreception in Birds," published in the Biophysical Journal in 2000, presented a new paradigm for explaining life phenomena. Led by the research team of Thorsten Ritz, Salih Adem, and Klaus Schulten at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, this paper detailed and formulated the radical pair mechanism driven by light absorption as the fundamental principle of biological magnetoreception, opening the prelude to full-fledged quantum biology. ■ Quantum Spin Awakened by Light Becomes a Compass The research team focused on the microscopic processes inside photoreceptor proteins (pointing to cryptochromes as a strong candidate) present in the avian retina. Electron radical pairs generated inside the protein by absorbing light maintain a state of quantum entanglement and respond to the extremely minute external perturbation of the Earth's magnetic field. The research team proved with a 3D mathematical model that this radical pair oscillates between singlet and triplet spin states due to anisotropic hyperfine coupling, and that its reaction yield distinctly varies depending on its alignment with the external magnetic field. ■ Quantum Mechanical Signals Converted into Visual Information The difference in the ratio of singlet and triplet spin states, which varies according to the direction of the Earth's magnetic field, is ultimately converted into a macroscopic biochemical signal that affects the visual transduction pathway. Through this, the bird sees three-dimensional visual modulation patterns corresponding to the direction of the magnetic field superimposed on its field of vision, which acts as a biological compass, according to the research team's explanation. This groundbreaking theory strongly suggested that living organisms overcome the thermal noise of body temperature and actively utilize microscopic quantum mechanical principles in their sensory organs, providing the most important physical foundation for the subsequent explosive development of avian magnetoreception mechanisms and modern quantum biology. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10653784/

title: Ending the Long Debate on the Migratory Bird's Compass... Emergence of a 'Radical Pair + Magnetite' Hybrid Quantum Sensor Model
abstract: · Published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS), proposing a hybrid compass mechanism that integrates two competing hypotheses for avian magnetoreception. · Magnetite nanoparticles amplify the weak Earth's magnetic field, and the radical pairs in cryptochromes decode directional information by responding to the intensity of the amplified magnetic field. · Demonstrated that it can overcome the limitations of the existing standard radical pair model and dramatically improve magnetic detection sensitivity by 10 to potentially 100-fold. [Quantum Biology Society] The academic community has long been divided by two hypotheses regarding the principle of the biological compass that allows night-migratory songbirds to accurately navigate while flying tens of thousands of kilometers. One is the radical pair chemical reaction of cryptochrome proteins in the retina, and the other involves crystals of inorganic magnetic minerals such as intracellular magnetite. Recently, a groundbreaking study was published that seamlessly combines these two competing hypotheses, breaking through the fatal limitations of existing models. A paper titled "A hybrid compass mechanism combining radical pairs and magnetite crystals" was published in the international journal, Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS). P. J. Hore, the sole author of this study, unravels the mystery of magnetoreception from a new perspective. ■ The Fatal Limitation of the Standard Radical Pair Model: Insufficient Sensitivity The existing standard radical pair model has been considered an attractive quantum biological mechanism for detecting the Earth's magnetic field, but it carried a crucial weakness. The effect of the weak Earth's magnetic field, which is only about 50 microtesla (μT), on the directionality of the radical pair reaction is too small, resulting in insufficient sensitivity to function as an actual biological navigation system. This is because complex internal interactions within the protein greatly dilute the magnetic response signal. ■ The Meeting of a Magnetite Antenna and a Quantum Sensor: The 'Hybrid Compass' To solve this problem, P. J. Hore proposed a hybrid mechanism where organic proteins and inorganic magnetic particles create synergy. In this model, the magnetic nanoparticle responds to and aligns with the weak Earth's magnetic field, creating a much stronger magnetic field of about 5 millitesla (mT) in its vicinity. In other words, magnetite acts as a powerful local amplifier that amplifies the weak Earth's magnetic field. At this time, instead of directly detecting the tiny directional dependence of the 50 μT Earth's magnetic field, the radical pairs inside cryptochrome decode directional information by responding to the intensity of the much stronger magnetic field generated by the magnetite nanoparticles. ■ Over 100-fold Sensitivity Improvement... Opening the Path for Experimental Validation As a result of the analysis, this hybrid sensor was shown to potentially provide 10 to 100-fold greater sensitivity than the standard radical pair model alone, which directly detects the tiny dependence on the direction of the Earth's magnetic field. This physicochemical framework, demonstrating how proteins (organic matter) and inorganic crystals cooperate, is being evaluated as the most realistic and powerful quantum biological model explaining the spectacular navigation of migratory birds.

title: Frequency Limit Disrupting the Migratory Bird's Compass... Demonstration of the 110-120MHz Grey Zone
abstract: A study published on bioRxiv demonstrated through behavioral experiments on Eurasian blackcaps the effect of the 110–120 MHz band, the predicted upper cut-off frequency for radiofrequency (RF) field disruption in the radical pair mechanism (RPM) that explains avian magnetic sensing. It confirmed a "grey zone" phenomenon where the magnetic orientation ability significantly decreases when electron spins of FAD-based radical pairs inside the cryptochrome (ErCry4a) protein are exposed to a broadband RF field of a specific band due to hyperfine interactions. By behaviorally proving the impact of broadband RF fields on biological radical pair reactions, it presents strong physical evidence capable of reverse-tracking the quantum characteristics of radicals existing inside living organisms. [Quantum Biology Society] The most promising hypothesis explaining the principle by which migratory birds sense the Earth's minute magnetic field to travel thousands of kilometers is the radical pair mechanism (RPM) based on quantum mechanics. This mechanism can be interfered with by external electromagnetic waves, and it was recently revealed through actual behavioral experiments that a specific frequency band is a physical cut-off limit that can disrupt the navigation of migratory birds. The paper titled "Magnetic compass orientation behaviour of Eurasian blackcaps at the predicted 110-120 MHz upper cut-off frequency for radiofrequency-field effects," published on the preprint repository bioRxiv, is a major study that demonstrated the exact frequency range in which the biological compass of migratory birds is disrupted. An international joint research team, including Professor Bo Leberecht and Professor Henrik Mouritsen from the Carl von Ossietzky Universität Oldenburg in Germany, and Professor P. J. Hore from the University of Oxford in the UK, spearheaded this innovative research. ■ Radical Pairs in Cryptochrome and Frequency Interference The operation of the quantum compass begins inside the cryptochrome (ErCry4a) protein present in the retina of migratory birds. Upon receiving light, the FAD cofactor is photoexcited, triggering a series of electron transfers and forming a radical pair with an unpaired electron spin. According to physical theory, it has been predicted that if this FAD-based radical pair is exposed to a radiofrequency (RF) field in the 100-120 MHz band due to internal hyperfine interactions' energy scales, its quantum spin dynamics will experience disruption. ■ Demonstration of the 110-120MHz Grey Zone To verify this theoretical prediction, the research team conducted precise behavioral experiments on Eurasian blackcap migratory birds. Experimental results showed that migratory birds exposed to broadband electromagnetic waves in the 110–120 MHz band, the predicted upper cut-off frequency, exhibited a significant decrease in their normal magnetic orientation capabilities. In other words, it was clearly proven that this specific frequency band acts as a so-called "grey zone" that neutralizes the compass of migratory birds. ■ Reverse-Tracking in vivo Quantum Characteristics Beyond simple behavioral observation, this experiment confirmed that a broadband RF field of a specific band has a destructive impact on biological radical pair reactions. Such behavioral demonstration of the cut-off frequency is extremely strong physical evidence supporting the actual existence of a radical pair system reliant on quantum spin states inside living organisms. This is evaluated as a monumental achievement that not only establishes criteria for environmental electromagnetic waves that disrupt the biological compass of migratory birds, but also backwards reverse-tracks microscopic quantum mechanical characteristics through the behavior of macroscopic living organisms. https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.05.21.726905v1.full

title: Tightly Bound Radical Pairs Also Feel Magnetic Fields
abstract: • Published in the international journal Nature Communications, elucidating that the magnetosensitivity of tightly bound radical pairs in cryptochrome is enabled by the quantum Zeno effect. • Overturns the limitations of the traditional radical pair mechanism (RPM), proving that even closely spaced and strongly interacting radical pairs can respond to weak magnetic fields at the level of the Earth's magnetic field if the recombination reaction is highly asymmetric. • Suggests that closely associated radical pairs involving superoxide may play a significant role in biological magnetosensing, providing a new physical answer to the mechanism of avian magnetoreception. [Quantum Biology Society] The fundamental operating principle of the biological compass—the secret to how migratory birds fly thousands of kilometers without getting lost—has been newly revealed. In conventional quantum biology, it has been considered that only distantly separated, weakly interacting electrons can sense the Earth's minute magnetic field. However, groundbreaking research has recently been published showing that even tightly bound radical pairs can act as excellent magnetic sensors under specific quantum mechanical conditions, drawing the attention of the academic community. Recently, a paper titled "Magnetosensitivity of tightly bound radical pairs in cryptochrome is enabled by the quantum Zeno effect" was published in the international journal Nature Communications. A research team including Matt C. J. Denton and Daniel R. Kattnig from the University of Exeter in the UK proposed a new magnetosensing model for closely bound biological radical pairs through this paper. ■ A Reversal of Tight Binding Overturning Traditional Limitations The most prominent hypothesis explaining biological magnetosensing is the radical pair mechanism (RPM). In this mechanism, magnetic field sensitivity has traditionally been known to occur only in radical pairs where the two electrons are far apart and interact very weakly. Conversely, in the case of closely bound pairs where the two electrons are too close, it was considered that strong exchange interactions halt the spin dynamics, rendering them completely unresponsive to weak magnetic fields like the Earth's. The researchers challenged this conventional notion through the FAD-superoxide radical pair within cryptochrome, a protein present in the avian retina that is strongly suspected to be a biological magnetic sensor. ■ The Magic of Asymmetry, the Quantum Zeno Effect As a result of the analysis, the researchers discovered a highly intriguing fact. Even for tightly bound radical pairs, if the recombination reaction that returns them to their original state is strongly asymmetric, they can distinctly respond to weak magnetic fields equivalent to the Earth's magnetic field strength. The research team revealed that the quantum Zeno effect intervenes at the base of this phenomenon. The quantum Zeno effect refers to a phenomenon where the state change of a quantum system is delayed or halted when it is continuously observed (or interacts strongly with its surrounding environment). The asymmetric reaction dynamics act as a kind of continuous interaction, maintaining the spin dynamics of the closely bound electron pair in a state that can respond sensitively to the Earth's magnetic field. ■ A New Paradigm in Biological Magnetosensing This study presents a completely new and plausible mechanism for how weak magnetic field effects occur in nature. In particular, it strongly suggests that closely associated radical pairs based on superoxide, which were considered unsuitable candidates for magnetic sensors in existing models, can also perform an important magnetosensing role in vivo. Experts evaluate that this discovery will serve as a crucial milestone in resolving existing debates about cryptochrome-mediated animal magnetoreception mechanisms and proving how biological sensor systems utilize the minute physical laws of quantum m

title: Proofreading Is Error Correction: Kinetic Proofreading, the Forge ISA, and Why Biology Achieves Near-Threshold Fidelity in a Thermal Bath
abstract: We show that kinetic proofreading (Hopfield 1974; Ninio 1975) and quantum error correction (QEC) are the same mathematical structure implemented in chemical and quantum hardware respectively. The identification is exact: the proofreading checkpoint is the QEC syndrome measurement; excision and re-synthesis are the correction operation; the logical information carrier (the nascent polymer sequence) is the logical qubit. The Forge ISA thermal parameter beta* is the proofreading operating point. Biological fidelity machines achieve near-threshold fidelity because natural selection drives beta* from both sides: below threshold the machine errors faster than it corrects (lethal); above threshold the machine wastes energy on unnecessary corrections (costly). The H^k complexity ladder predicts the number of proofreading levels: H^0 (geometric rejection, no energy cost), H^1 (kinetic proofreading, one irreversible energy step), H^2 (post-replication mismatch repair, multi-enzyme circuit). Fidelity per level is 10^3; total fidelity is the product over active levels. DNA polymerase~III (H^0 + H^1 + H^2) achieves 10^9; RNA polymerase (H^0 + H^1) achieves 10^6; the ribosome (3H^0 + H^1) achieves 10^4.

title: Quantum-Classical Hybrid Reduces Computational Cost by 20x... Revolutionizing AI Drug Discovery
abstract: • A study published on arXiv presents a hybrid framework combining VQE, QM/MM, and statistical thermodynamics to predict protein-ligand binding free energies (BFEs) accurately and rapidly. • Explicitly handles polarization, charge redistribution, and electron correlation—often missed by purely classical methods—achieving an outstanding mean absolute error (MAE) of 1.10 kcal/mol. • Reduces computational costs 20-fold compared to conventional alchemical free energy perturbation (FEP) approaches (taking approximately 25 minutes per ligand), establishing a robust foundation for high-throughput lead optimization and future ML-integrated screening. [Quantum Biology Society] Predicting protein-ligand binding free energy (BFE), a core challenge in drug discovery, has long faced a dilemma: increasing accuracy requires massive amounts of computing resources. Traditional high-precision techniques, such as free energy perturbation (FEP), are computationally intensive and difficult to scale for large-scale applications. Recently, a groundbreaking study has been published that breaks through these limitations using a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm based on a variational quantum eigensolver (VQE), reducing computational costs by 20-fold and drawing intense interest from the pharmaceutical industry. ■ Perfect Synergy Between Quantum Mechanics and Statistical Thermodynamics The study titled "Synergistic Computational Approaches for Accelerated Drug Discovery: Integrating Quantum Mechanics, Statistical Thermodynamics, and Quantum Computing," released on the preprint repository arXiv, combines the advantages of classical and quantum computing. A research team led by Farzad Molani and Art E. Cho (from inCerebro and Korea University) designed a new framework that maximizes both computational efficiency and precision. The researchers integrated quantum mechanically refined ligand partial charges and QM/MM interaction evaluations based on the Mining Minima sampling technique. Notably, they added an electronic energy correction step using a variational quantum eigensolver (VQE), significantly boosting precision without compromising efficiency. ■ Overcoming the Blind Spots of Polarization and Electron Correlation The greatest strength of this hybrid model is its ability to explicitly handle effects such as polarization, charge redistribution, and electron correlation, which are frequently underestimated in purely classical scoring systems. By applying this system to 23 protein targets and 543 diverse ligands, the team successfully achieved an excellent MAE of 1.10 kcal/mol along with high fidelity in ranking predictions. ■ 20x Faster Computation: A Cornerstone for Machine Learning (ML) Integration The most encouraging achievement is the overwhelming computational speed. Using standard computing resources, the workflow completed calculations in approximately 25 minutes per ligand, representing a 20-fold reduction in costs compared to traditional FEP methods. The researchers emphasized that this level of accuracy and efficiency is highly suitable for the high-throughput lead optimization process in pharmaceutical drug discovery. Furthermore, this framework is expected to provide an excellent basis for realizing large-scale adaptive predictive screening strategies through seamless integration with future machine learning (ML) models.

title: Proton Transfer Causing DNA Point Mutations: Quantum Tunneling Effects in G-C Base Pairs Revealed
abstract: A study published in the international journal PCCP (Physical Chemistry Chemical Physics) models the proton transfer pathways and quantum tunneling rates during Watson-Crick tautomerism in DNA A-T and G-C base pairs, utilizing Density Functional Theory (DFT) and a machine learning-based Nudged Elastic Band (ML-NEB) algorithm. The research physically demonstrates that proton transfer in A-T base pairs is highly unstable due to an extremely low reverse-reaction barrier, making the occurrence of mutations during the replication process highly improbable. In G-C base pairs, a high quantum tunneling correction value was observed, providing the first physical proof that the mutant form (G-C) possesses a biological lifespan sufficient to be misread as an error by the human DNA replication machinery (replisome). [Quantum Biology Society] The phenomenon wherein protons shift positions within the hydrogen bonds of DNA—the carrier of an organism's genetic information—can induce transient but lethal point mutations. Known as tautomerism, this process has long been identified as a potential root cause of genetic variation and oncogenesis. A recent study published in Physical Chemistry Chemical Physics (PCCP) has combined pure quantum mechanical modeling (DFT) with machine learning techniques to precisely calculate the energy barriers of double proton transfer occurring within A-T and G-C base pairs. This significant physicochemical research was conducted by L. Slocombe, J. S. Al-Khalili, and M. Sacchi of the University of Surrey, UK. ■ Instantly Collapsing A-T Tautomers vs. Surviving G-C Tautomers The research team analyzed the energy landscape as the hydrogen bond structure shifts from the standard (amino-keto) to the mutant (imino-enol) form, using Density Functional Theory (DFT) and a machine learning-based Nudged Elastic Band (ML-NEB) algorithm. The results revealed that in A-T base pairs, although a quantum tunneling effect transitioning toward the mutant A*-T* state was observed, the reverse-reaction barrier was virtually nonexistent. Consequently, the state could not be maintained and immediately collapsed back into its original standard form. The situation was different for G-C base pairs. Beyond the classical reaction where protons cross the barrier using only thermal energy at room temperature, it was confirmed that wave-like movement via quantum tunneling contributes decisively to the formation of mutant populations. The team mathematically proved that the G-C mutant (G*-C*), which showed a significantly high tunneling correction value, possesses a lifespan long enough to reach the human replisome, suggesting a high probability of solidifying into a permanent point mutation. ■ Genetic Stability Governed by Physical Laws This study is a landmark achievement, demonstrating that complex DNA mutations occurring during the most critical replication processes of life are not merely the result of random thermal fluctuations, but are substantially controlled and shaped by the microscopic physical laws of quantum tunneling dynamics. By providing objective data on how the wave-like nature of protons physically threatens the fidelity of genetic information replication, this study underscores the importance of a quantum biological approach in future research regarding DNA damage and mutation-related diseases. #DNAPointMutation #ProtonTransfer #QuantumTunneling #WatsonCrickTautomerism #BasePair #QuantumBiology #Biophysics #GeneticMutation #MolecularDynamics #KoreanSocietyOfQuantumBiology https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2021/cp/d0cp05781a

title: Quantum Sampling Architecture for Protein Structure Reconstruction on Utility-Scale Hardware
abstract: Predicting the structure of short peptides in protein binding pockets remains difficult because this regime requires physics-based conformational search, yet existing methods do not provide a practical way to carry out that search on current hardware. We present QSAD, a quantum-classical framework that reformulates peptide structure prediction as amino-acid-level Hamiltonian sampling and replaces iterative optimization with non-iterative Hamiltonian evolution. Executed entirely on IBM Heron R2 across 101 binding-pocket peptides (5-18 residues), QSAD improves prediction accuracy by 27-71% over all evaluated AI and quantum baselines while maintaining the lowest variance across tested lengths. QSAD also tolerates noise levels 3-5x beyond typical hardware error rates, where iterative methods fail, and reduces mean quantum execution time by 27x relative to VQE. The sampled ensemble further supports approximate reconstruction of protein energy landscapes. These results establish coarse-grained quantum sampling as a practical computational path for structure prediction in regimes where data-driven methods lack sufficient signal.

title: Probing Extended Recognition Sites in Zn-Metalloproteins via Quantum Chemistry and Polarizable Molecular Dynamics
abstract: Zn-metalloproteins play vital roles in numerous metabolic processes, making them high-value targets for structure-based drug design. To advance these efforts, it is useful to unravel the individual components of the intermolecular interaction energies (DE) that stabilize the Znbinding cavity, both in the absence and presence of bound protein ligands. Here, we utilize quantum chemistry (QC) to decompose DE into distinct physical contributions. The relative magnitudes of these components vary significantly depending on the coordination number (four to six) and the chemical nature ('hard' vs. 'soft') of the Zn-coordinating ligands. These high-level QC analyses serve to calibrate and validate polarizable molecular mechanics potentials, effectively extending the accurate description of electronic effects beyond the immediate Zn-binding cavity to enlarged recognition sites and, ultimately, entire protein systems over long molecular dynamics (MD) simulation timescales. Following a concise overview of our QC methodology, we present validation studies on complexes containing up to 300 atoms and discuss the prospects of applying this framework to large-scale simulations of Zn-metalloprotein-ligand complexes. Finally, the structural and energetic role of discrete, highly polarizable water molecules is highlighted.

title: 50. YUCT First Principles and Complete Derivation
abstract: The Yakushev Unified Coordination Theory (YUCT) establishes a hierarchical coordination network as the fundamental structure of reality, governed by the ontological first principle K_eff > 1. From a single axiom (fractal scale invariance) and the definitions of the YPSDC protocol, three theorems are proven: (1) minimal dictionary entropy is exactly two bits, (2) spatial dimension is necessarily three, and (3) time emerges as a measure of coordination imperfection. The universal error law ε ∝ K_eff^{-2/3} is empirically validated across more than a dozen independent experiments spanning atomic, biological, geophysical, and cosmological scales. From this law the algebraic loop S_odd = 6/5 (1.2) and S_even = 4/5 (0.8) is derived, fixing the universal scaling exponent β = 2/3. The scaling quantum q = (3/2)^{1/3} ≈ 1.1447 generates a universal mass ladder from the Planck scale to living cells, with charged fermion masses following m_f = m_e·q^{N_f} (N_f half-integer). The fine-structure constant α^{-1} ≈ 137.036 is obtained from the topological invariant N_gauge = 12 on the compact fibre F_18 with universal correction βγ = 0.20. The cosmological constant Ω_Λ = 2/3 follows from the same topology, and the electroweak scale emerges from the Weyl fermion count L = 96. The theory contains no adjustable continuous parameters and makes several concrete, falsifiable predictions across physics, biology, and cosmology. This metadata accompanies the complete multilingual documentation (English, Russian, German, Chinese, Arabic, Japanese, Korean) of YUCT First Principles and Complete Derivation, version.

title: Unraveling the Mysteries of Quantum Mechanics… The Encounter Between 'Quantum Darwinism' and Biology
abstract: ∙ Quanta Magazine analyzes the principles of macroscopic world formation through quantum decoherence and quantum Darwinism. ∙ 'Einselection' fixes specific quantum states… similar to how life preserves information. ∙ Integrating quantum biology from physical and philosophical perspectives, redefining the fundamental principles of life's information processing. [Quantum Biology Society] An in-depth analysis has been proposed on how the bizarre phenomena of quantum mechanics transform into the solid macroscopic reality we observe, and what connection this process has with the evolution of life. Recently, the science media outlet Quanta Magazine reported through a contribution by renowned science writer Philip Ball that the theories of 'Quantum Darwinism' and 'Decoherence,' pioneered by physicist Wojciech Zurek, are the key to connecting biological systems with the macroscopic world. ■ The Reality of 'Survival of the Fittest' Created by 'Einselection' In the quantum realm, particles can exist in multiple states simultaneously (superposition), but in the macroscopic world we observe, they appear in only one definite state. According to Dr. Zurek's theory, this happens because quantum systems continuously interact with their surrounding environment, undergoing a process of 'decoherence' where information is dissipated. The core of this process is 'Einselection' (Environment-Induced Superselection). The environment does not accommodate all states; instead, it selects only the most stable and predictable states, known as 'pointer states,' and replicates them innumerably within the environment. Just as only the genetic information advantageous for survival is passed on and spreads in natural selection, the essence of 'Quantum Darwinism' is that quantum information also undergoes a 'survival of the fittest' process dictated by the environment. This ultimately forms an objective macroscopic reality that all of us can commonly perceive. ■ Life, a Product of Evolution that Anchors Quantum Information into Reality Philip Ball noted that these physical principles are strikingly similar to the way biological systems process information. He explains that the vital activities of living organisms—such as recognizing external stimuli, preserving genetic information, and controlling complex metabolic processes—are fundamentally the very processes of filtering meaningful information from quantum superpositions and anchoring them into the physical states of the real world. This report suggests that quantum biology is not merely a discipline that investigates microscopic reactions within cells. Rather, it is a grand unified theory explaining how life has utilized the universe's fundamental principles to process information and evolve. Built upon the theories of Dr. Zurek, a master of modern physics, this analysis presents a new philosophical and physical paradigm that views life as a cosmic entity where matter, information, and quantum mechanics are perfectly integrated.

title: Metabolic Enzyme as a 'Quantum Logic Gate'? ... Unraveling the Cause of Diabetic Complications with Quantum Mechanics
abstract: • Raised the hypothesis that 'TIM', a pivotal enzyme in cellular glycolysis, functions as a 'quantum logic gate' beyond a simple chemical catalyst. • Elucidated that upon the occurrence of quantum decoherence, methylglyoxal, a toxic substance causing diabetic complications, is produced. • Reinterpreted the root cause of metabolic diseases as a 'failure of quantum interference', and demonstrated the protective effects of SGLT2 inhibitors through a quantum model. [Quantum Biology Society] Groundbreaking research results have emerged suggesting that metabolic enzymes, which generate energy in our bodies, operate not merely as simple chemicals, but like 'quantum logic gates', the computational units of quantum computers. Recently, a paper titled "Triosephosphate Isomerase as a Quantum Logic Gate: Could quantum decoherence be toxic?" was published on bioRxiv, a preprint repository for biology. This study is drawing extraordinary attention from the medical community by reinterpreting the pathogenesis of diseases from a quantum mechanical perspective, going beyond classical biochemistry. ■ A Key Enzyme in Life Phenomena Operates as a 'Quantum Logic Gate' The research team focused on the operational mechanism of 'triosephosphate isomerase (TIM)', a pivotal enzyme in cellular glycolysis that breaks down glucose into energy. The researchers modeled the precise proton transfer process occurring within the enzyme as a 'quantum tunneling' action rather than a simple chemical catalytic reaction. This is an unconventional hypothesis that the TIM enzyme functions like a 'quantum logic gate', the fundamental unit of a quantum computer, to control the conversion of substances with extreme precision. ■ Quantum Errors (Decoherence) Create 'Toxicity' The most surprising discovery is what happens when inefficiencies arise due to the so-called 'quantum decoherence' phenomenon during this quantum tunneling process. The study showed that when an error occurs in the computation (catalysis) as the quantum state breaks down, a fatal toxic byproduct called 'methylglyoxal' is produced instead of normal metabolites, inducing the formation of advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that cause diabetic complications. In other words, a 'quantum mechanical malfunction' (failure of quantum tunneling) of metabolic enzymes in cells can be the direct cause of metabolic disease complications. ■ The Efficacy of the Diabetes Drug 'SGLT2 Inhibitors' Proven by a Quantum Model... A New Paradigm in Drug Development Furthermore, the research team developed a mathematical quantum model of how 'SGLT2 inhibitors', a widely used diabetes treatment, protect the body. As a result of simulation analysis through a Python script, it was confirmed that this drug exerts a protective effect on vascular and renal cells by lowering the probability of the formation of the toxic intermediate (methylglyoxal). This approach is highly significant in that it reinterpreted the root cause of metabolic diseases, including diabetes, as a problem at the level of 'quantum interference' rather than a simple chemical imbalance. This research, which identified the causes of diseases from a quantum mechanical perspective and theoretically proved the efficacy of drugs, is expected to provide a strong foundation for the development of new metabolic disease drugs with novel mechanisms that fundamentally block the generation of toxic byproducts in the future.

title: The Larger and More Flexible the Molecule, the Longer 'Quantum Coherence' Lasts
abstract: • Published in PNAS, elucidating that increasing the size and flexibility of a molecule's carbon skeleton actually reduces electronic decoherence and prolongs charge migration. • Analyzed the contributions of individual vibrational modes through ab initio semiclassical dynamics, confirming that only normal modes preserving molecular symmetry induce decoherence. • Explains the mechanism by which giant biopolymers maintain their quantum properties, presenting a new paradigm for designing functional molecules that resist decoherence in the future. [Quantum Biology Society] The long-standing common sense in modern physics—that the larger and more flexible a molecule is, the faster quantum coherence collapses due to surrounding environmental noise and complex internal vibrations—has been overturned. Recently, a paper titled "Can increasing the size and flexibility of a molecule reduce decoherence and prolong charge migration?" was published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS). A joint research team including Alan Scheidegger and Jiří J. L. Vaníček from the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne (EPFL), and Nikolay V. Golubev from the University of Arizona physically proved through this paper the surprising fact that as the size of a molecule increases, the lifespans of quantum coherence and charge migration are actually drastically prolonged. ■ The Paradox of Size and Flexibility, Prolonging Quantum Lifespan The research team performed ab initio semiclassical dynamics simulations on carbon skeleton extension models progressing from propynal to butynal and pentynal. It was expected that an increase in the size and flexibility of the molecule would lead to a faster collapse of electronic coherence due to an increased number of internal vibrational modes, but the results were exactly the opposite. A phenomenon was observed where the longer the carbon chain, the more distinct the charge migration became and the longer the coherent state lasted. ■ Mechanism of Decoherence Revealed by Vibrational Mode Decomposition To elucidate this paradoxical phenomenon, the researchers analyzed the overall decoherence by decomposing it into the contributions of individual vibrational modes. The analysis showed that the initial stage of decoherence is caused by momentum separation of the nuclear wavepackets driven by high- and intermediate-frequency vibrations, after which the coherence completely disappears due to position separation in the low-frequency modes. A particularly notable discovery is that only vibrational modes preserving the molecule's symmetry induce decoherence. As the size of the molecule increased, the decoherence contributions of specific vibrational modes, such as C=O stretching vibrations or H rockings, diminished, and hole-mixing increased, thereby improving overall charge migration. ■ Quantum Survival Methods of Biopolymers This study overcomes the existing macroscopic limitation that larger and more complex systems easily lose their quantum properties, explaining the physical defense mechanisms of how biopolymers can independently maintain their quantum characteristics for a long time. The researchers' findings are expected to provide a completely new paradigm in the fields of attochemistry and quantum biology for developing robust artificial enzymes and functional molecules that resist quantum decoherence by intelligently designing molecular structures.

title: Glycolytic Enzyme as a Quantum Logic Gate... Hidden Mechanism of Diabetes Drug Unlocked
abstract: • Published on bioRxiv, presenting a biophysical hypothesis that models triosephosphate isomerase (TIM), an enzyme essential for cellular glucose metabolism, as a quantum logic gate. • Predicted through a quantum mechanical model that SGLT2 inhibitors (SGLT2i), a diabetes treatment, reduce the probability of quantum decoherence in the TIM enzyme reaction. • Pioneering a new research frontier of quantum pharmacology by preventing the production of methylglyoxal, a cytotoxic byproduct, through the suppression of decoherence. [Quantum Biology Society] Groundbreaking biophysical research has been published revealing that an enzyme essential for cellular glucose metabolism (glycolysis) virtually operates like a quantum logic gate, the computational unit of a quantum computer. Going beyond classical biochemistry, this is a milestone achievement interpreting the human protective mechanisms of metabolic disease treatments from the perspective of subatomic quantum information processing. A paper titled "Triosephosphate Isomerase as a Quantum Logic Gate: Could quantum decoherence be toxic?" was released on the preprint repository bioRxiv. A joint research team of Daniele Romanello, a physician in Internal Medicine at Ospedale San Pietro Fatebenefratelli in Rome, Italy, and Andrea Romanello from the Department of Information Engineering at Sapienza University of Rome proposed this innovative quantum medical model. ■ Enzyme Catalysis Interpreted as Quantum Computation The researchers focused on the catalytic process of triosephosphate isomerase (TIM), a key enzyme in glycolysis, converting dihydroxyacetone phosphate (DHAP) to glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (G3P). Instead of viewing this process merely as a classical "lock-and-key" model where specific molecular structures fit together, they remodeled it as a highly advanced quantum computational workflow involving precise proton transfer. This implies that enzymes in vivo act as logic circuits that autonomously control quantum states to derive specific biochemical outcomes. ■ SGLT2 Inhibitors Protect Cells by Preventing Quantum Decoherence What is even more interesting is that this quantum model interpreted the hidden human protective effects of sodium-glucose co-transporter 2 inhibitors (SGLT2i), which are widely prescribed as treatments for heart failure and diabetes. According to the model's predictions, if quantum decoherence occurs—where the enzyme loses its quantum state due to interference from the external environment during the metabolic process—it produces methylglyoxal, a toxic byproduct that destroys cells and strongly accelerates aging. As a result of the researchers' analysis, SGLT2i drugs were found to significantly reduce the probability of quantum decoherence in this TIM enzyme reaction. In other words, by suppressing the noise in the intracellular quantum computation process, the drug dramatically lowers the accumulation of the highly toxic substance methylglyoxal, ultimately exerting a powerful organ-protective effect. ■ The Prelude to the Era of Quantum Pharmacology This study holds great significance in that it reinterprets the pathogenesis of diseases and the mechanism of drug action beyond molecular chemical bonds, from the perspective of quantum coherence and dynamics. Experts anticipate that this approach will serve as an excellent starting point for quantum pharmacology, bringing a fundamental paradigm shift to the design of next-generation drugs for treating metabolic syndrome and degenerative diseases.

title: Posner Molecules as Quantum Cognition Mediators in the Brain: Unveiling Nuclear Spin Entanglement and the Quantum Mechanical Se
abstract: A study published in the international journal Scientific Reports analyzes a brain quantum cognition model of Posner molecules based on nuclear spin, which is significantly less sensitive to decoherence than electron spin in biological systems. It reveals that phosphorus (P) nuclei are prepared in a spin-entangled state and subsequently protected by being enclosed within a spin-zero calcium nuclear cage, and that this entanglement can directly modulate the release of calcium ions and neural activation through the hydrolysis of the molecules. It confirms that when lithium isotopes, used as a treatment for bipolar disorder, are doped into Posner molecules, the quantum mechanical entanglement and coherence dynamics of the nuclear spins are fundamentally altered, presenting a physical possibility that the brain utilizes protected neural entanglement as an information processing resource. [Quantum Biology Society] Most research identifying the role of quantum spin in biological systems has focused on electron spins, such as the geomagnetic compass of migratory birds. However, nuclear spin models, which possess significantly longer decoherence times and are thus more suitable for biological processes, are emerging as a powerful hypothesis to explain the brain's quantum cognition mechanisms. Among them, the most prominent candidate is Posner molecules, which are calcium phosphate clusters. A recent study titled "Entanglement and coherence in pure and doped Posner molecules," published in the Nature Portfolio journal Scientific Reports, rigorously analyzes the protective mechanism of spin entanglement occurring inside these Posner molecules and the effects of lithium isotopes on quantum resources. A joint research team led by Dr. Betony Adams and Professor Francesco Petruccione of the University of KwaZulu-Natal in South Africa announced this innovative research. ■ Phosphorus (P) Nuclear Spin Entanglement Protected by a Calcium Cage According to the quantum cognition hypothesis, phosphorus (P) nuclei generated during metabolic processes are prepared in a spin-entangled state. When these entangled phosphates are assembled into Posner molecules composed of spin-zero calcium nuclei, the entangled state is perfectly shielded from external thermal noise and magnetic interference. The researchers mathematically confirmed that as the spin-spin coupling (J-coupling) strength within the Posner molecule increases, the coherence and entanglement of the phosphorus nuclei are directly enhanced. This quantum entanglement, protected within the molecular symmetry, subsequently influences the binding and hydrolysis rates of Posner molecules, directly regulating the release of free calcium ions that trigger neural activation. ■ Lithium Doping and Alterations in Quantum Dynamics The researchers further investigated the quantum changes that occur when lithium isotopes (⁶Li, ⁷Li), which are known to exert distinct effects on animal behavior, are doped into the central calcium sites of Posner molecules. The analysis revealed that while both lithium isotopes reduced the absolute magnitude of entanglement and coherence itself, they fundamentally altered the period of the phosphorus nuclei's quantum spin dynamics. This strongly suggests a potential quantum mechanical mechanism of action where lithium suppresses neural hyperexcitation in bipolar disorder by altering the spin-dependence of free calcium ion production within brain neurons. ■ Entangled Subspaces Preserved by the Biological Environment In particular, this study demonstrated that an intriguing quantum mechanical protection occurs even under environmental interference where the entangled phosphate binds to protons (hydrogen nuclear spins) instead of calcium. Under environments like the Earth's magnetic field, which induces a Zeeman energy difference greater than the spin coupling force, unentangled triplet states become energetically isolated from the entangled states. title: Quantum Walk of Mitochondrial Complex I... Revealing the Hidden Bottleneck of Electron Transport
abstract: A study published on bioRxiv identified a conserved structural bottleneck at the N5-N6a interface within mitochondrial complex I that suppresses direct electron transfer. By applying continuous-time quantum-walk flux analysis, it demonstrated that this bottleneck does not simply limit electron flow, but plays a crucial role in bypassing and redistributing it into alternative pathways mediated by amino acid residues. This organization of electron flow persists perfectly even under environmental decoherence conditions, representing an evolutionarily conserved mechanism which shows that geometric constraints encoded in the protein structure govern the entire quantum transport within the biochemical network. [Quantum Biology Society] Complex I in mitochondria, the cell's energy factory, is the first gateway of the electron transport chain essential for sustaining life. Recent dazzling advancements in structural biology have made it possible to precisely compare and analyze the 3D structures of this massive and complex protein complex across diverse species, from bacteria to mammals. The paper titled "Quantum transport in mitochondrial complex I is governed by a conserved structural bottleneck," published on the preprint repository bioRxiv, revealed the surprising secrets of electron flow occurring inside this complex through quantum mechanical analysis. A domestic joint research team led by Dr. Ji-Yong Sung from the Department of Neurosurgery at Seoul National University Bundang Hospital and Professor Jae-Ho Cheong from the Department of Surgery at Yonsei University College of Medicine and the Department of Quantum Information Science at the Graduate School spearheaded this innovative research. ■ A Structural Bottleneck Redistributing Electrons The research team analyzed the iron-sulfur (Fe-S) cluster network inside complex I across 10 species and confirmed the existence of an evolutionarily conserved structural bottleneck at the N5 and N6a interface that strongly suppresses the direct transfer of electrons. Surprisingly, this bottleneck was not merely an obstacle blocking the flow of electrons. When the research team applied a cutting-edge physical technique called continuous-time quantum-walk flux analysis, they found that instead of blocking the electron flow, this bottleneck played a decisive role in efficiently bypassing and redistributing the electrons into alternative pathways mediated by surrounding amino acid residues. ■ Evolutionary Code Overcoming Decoherence Even more interestingly, these alternative pathways are strongly conserved across multiple species through structural motifs composed of cysteine (Cys) and proline (Pro), and they stably support a substantial amount of flux that is comparable to or exceeds the direct pathway in actual electron transport. This sophisticated behavior of electron flow persisted perfectly even under environmental decoherence conditions, where quantum states are disrupted by the warm and disordered environment inside the cell. This is a groundbreaking discovery proving that life did not simply pursue the efficiency of quantum transport, but has perfectly controlled and governed the entire electron flow within the biochemical network quantum mechanically by encoding geometric constraints into the macroscopic structure of proteins. https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.05.28.728423v1
